title: Workspace and repository bootstrap
description:
## Background
The repository is currently plan-heavy and pre-implementation. The planned Rust workspace, crates, and test skeletons do not exist yet.

## Objective
Create the project skeleton needed for all Rust implementation work.

## Why this matters
Every downstream issue depends on a stable repository layout, dependency declaration strategy, and crate ownership model.

## In scope
- root Cargo workspace
- crate scaffolding for core/indexer/store/search
- supporting directories for tests, fixtures, benches, docs
- repository hygiene required for iterative development

## Out of scope
- feature implementation inside crates beyond scaffolding

## Success Criteria
- workspace builds as empty scaffolding
- crate responsibilities match PLAN.md
- directory structure supports planned tests/docs/fixtures
- the ready queue points contributors at concrete child tasks rather than the epic itself

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Reasoning: this epic is intentionally P0 because the repository currently has no Rust workspace or crate scaffold. Almost every planned implementation issue is blocked on making the planned structure real.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text:
Planning refinement: epic deferred so 📋 Ready work (1 issue with no blockers):

1. [● P0] [task] smart-grep-27a.1: Create root Cargo workspace configuration highlights executable child tasks rather than umbrella tracking issues.

title: Create root Cargo workspace configuration
description:
Objective:
Create the root Cargo workspace and shared workspace dependency declarations described in the plan.

Why this matters:
This establishes the canonical repository shape and dependency ownership model that all Rust work will share.

Planned files/modules:
- root `Cargo.toml`
- optional `Cargo.lock`

Acceptance criteria:
- workspace members are declared consistently with PLAN.md
- shared dependencies are centralized where intended

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Planning refinement: root workspace setup remains the main structural prerequisite, but it no longer unnecessarily serializes repository hygiene work that can safely happen in parallel.

title: Scaffold core/indexer/store/search crates
description:
Objective:
Create the planned crates for `core`, `indexer`, `store`, and `search`, each with base Cargo manifests and module entry points.

Why this matters:
This turns the plan's architectural boundaries into enforceable compiler-level boundaries.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on root workspace configuration

Acceptance criteria:
- all planned crates exist with minimal compile-ready skeletons
- crate names and responsibilities align with PLAN.md

title: Create supporting directories, fixtures, and ignore hygiene
description:
Objective:
Create supporting repository directories and hygiene files required for tests, fixtures, docs, benches, and local index data.

Why this matters:
A clean scaffold reduces future churn and lets tests/docs/benchmarks grow in the intended locations from the start.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on root workspace configuration

Acceptance criteria:
- planned directories such as `tests/`, `benches/`, `test-fixtures/`, and `docs/` exist as needed
- ignore rules cover `.smart-grep/` and other expected generated state

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Planning refinement: removed the hard dependency on the root workspace manifest. Directory scaffolding and ignore hygiene can proceed in parallel with Cargo workspace setup, which gives the project a better initial ready queue without introducing correctness risk.

title: Add test and benchmark skeletons
description:
Objective:
Add baseline integration-test and benchmark skeletons matching the planned project structure.

Why this matters:
The project should be test-first enough that feature work has a place to land immediately rather than inventing structure ad hoc later.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on crate scaffolding and supporting directories

Acceptance criteria:
- baseline test and benchmark entry points exist in the planned locations
- contributors can add new coverage without reorganizing the repo

title: Multi-language support expansion
description:
## Background
The MVP can start with one or two languages, but the product promise is semantic search across common polyglot repositories.

## Objective
Add and validate the target v1 language set with language-specific extraction rules.

## Why this matters
Cross-language retrieval is a core differentiator over conventional grep-based workflows.

## In scope
- extractor implementations for the planned v1 languages
- dispatcher wiring and fixture coverage
- language-specific metadata/test detection heuristics

## Out of scope
- future-language expansion beyond the v1 set

## Success Criteria
- supported languages are indexed with coherent chunks
- per-language tests demonstrate extraction quality
- language filters behave predictably
- the language rollout can proceed incrementally without destabilizing the MVP path

title: Implement JavaScript extractor
description:
Objective:
Implement the JavaScript chunk extractor and its language-specific heuristics.

Why this matters:
JavaScript is a common target language and a useful baseline for cross-language value.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on the initial extraction vertical slice and shared extractor abstractions

Acceptance criteria:
- JS functions/classes/components/routes are chunked according to plan heuristics
- tests cover common JS patterns

title: Implement TypeScript extractor
description:
Objective:
Implement the TypeScript chunk extractor and TS-specific metadata heuristics.

Why this matters:
TypeScript repos are a primary target for agent workflows and need support beyond plain JS behavior.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on JavaScript or shared JS/TS extraction foundations

Acceptance criteria:
- TS interfaces/types/functions/classes are handled coherently
- tests cover TS-specific syntax and metadata cases

title: Implement Rust extractor
description:
Objective:
Implement the Rust chunk extractor with Rust-specific symbol/test/impl heuristics.

Why this matters:
Rust is both a target language for users and the implementation language of smart-grep itself.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on the initial extraction vertical slice and extractor abstractions

Acceptance criteria:
- Rust functions, impls, traits, structs, enums, and test markers are extracted as planned
- tests cover Rust-specific node handling

title: Implement Python extractor
description:
Objective:
Implement the Python chunk extractor, including decorator/docstring/type-annotation handling where planned.

Why this matters:
Python support is necessary for the cross-language promise and exercises different metadata patterns than JS/Rust.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on shared extractor abstractions and the stable indexing pipeline

Acceptance criteria:
- Python functions/classes/decorated definitions are chunked coherently
- tests validate docstring and annotation treatment

title: Implement Ruby extractor
description:
Objective:
Implement the Ruby chunk extractor, including rescue/controller/action heuristics described in the plan.

Why this matters:
Ruby/Rails-style repositories are a strong use case for semantic retrieval over exact-match search.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on shared extractor abstractions and the stable indexing pipeline

Acceptance criteria:
- Ruby methods/modules/classes/rescue blocks are handled as planned
- tests cover Rails/RSpec-style heuristics where practical

title: Add multi-language fixtures and integration coverage
description:
Objective:
Add cross-language dispatcher coverage, fixtures, and integration tests for the full planned v1 set.

Why this matters:
Per-language implementations only become a product promise once they are exercised together.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on all language extractor tasks

Acceptance criteria:
- dispatcher recognizes the full planned language set
- integration tests demonstrate indexing/search across supported languages

title: Searchable MVP foundations
description:
## Background
The first durable milestone is a complete vertical slice: discover files, parse code into chunks, embed them locally, persist metadata/vectors, and answer a natural-language query.

## Objective
Ship the smallest trustworthy end-to-end semantic search path in Rust.

## Why this matters
This epic converts the idea into durable infrastructure and becomes the base for all later retrieval, explainability, graph, and intelligence features.

## In scope
- core data contracts and traits
- storage schema and ANN wrapper
- indexing pipeline
- query execution and CLI output
- baseline tests proving index -> search behavior

## Out of scope
- advanced ranking
- time-travel, sessions, lint, coverage, MCP

## Success Criteria
- a small repo can be indexed and queried successfully
- results include path/lines/score/preview
- fixtures validate meaning-based retrieval end to end
- child issues cover the critical path clearly enough that implementation can proceed without reopening architectural questions

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Reasoning: this epic is the load-bearing vertical slice. Do not scatter effort into advanced features before the end-to-end index -> search path is trustworthy and fixture-tested.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Planning refinement: epic deferred so contributors see concrete critical-path tasks in the ready queue instead of abstract coordination issues.

title: Define shared core data structures
description:
Objective:
Define the shared value types and enums that every crate relies on, including chunk identity, language representation, result shape, and confidence model scaffolding.

Why this matters:
Stable contracts are required before indexing, storage, or search logic can evolve coherently.

Planned files/modules:
- shared type definitions in the planned core/indexer public surface

Acceptance criteria:
- core data structures from PLAN.md are represented clearly
- type ownership is documented so later crates know what to import and extend

title: Implement baseline semantic retrieval and ranking
description:
Objective:
Implement query embedding, nearest-neighbor retrieval, baseline ranking, and result formatting.

Why this matters:
This is the core search experience users will judge the tool by.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on end-to-end indexing pipeline and ANN wrapper

Acceptance criteria:
- natural-language queries return ranked chunks with score/path/line metadata
- formatting is usable both for humans and later machine output modes

title: Implement CLI entry points for index and search
description:
Objective:
Add the core CLI commands and flags for indexing and search, including basic JSON output.

Why this matters:
The engine only becomes usable once contributors can drive it through a stable CLI surface.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on indexing and retrieval paths

Acceptance criteria:
- `smart-grep index` and search command paths work end to end
- planned MVP flags such as top-k/lang/json are wired coherently

title: Add end-to-end fixture-driven search tests
description:
Objective:
Create the first end-to-end tests and fixtures proving that semantic search finds conceptually relevant code that exact-match search misses.

Why this matters:
The project needs permanent evidence, not just anecdotal local runs.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on CLI and retrieval working end to end

Acceptance criteria:
- integration tests cover index -> search round trip
- fixtures encode the core product claims from the plan

title: Define core traits and error model
description:
Objective:
Define the `ChunkExtractor` and `Embedder` traits, plus error-handling conventions across crates.

Why this matters:
These traits preserve future optionality while keeping the MVP implementation simple and consistent.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on shared data structures

Acceptance criteria:
- extractor and embedder interfaces match planned responsibilities
- crate-level error conventions are documented and usable from the CLI crate

title: Implement canonical SQLite schema and migration layer
description:
Objective:
Create the canonical SQLite schema and DB open/migrate layer for all persistent metadata.

Why this matters:
The schema is the durable contract for indexing, retrieval, sessions, snapshots, and future intelligence features.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on shared types and trait decisions

Planned files/modules:
- `crates/store/src/schema.sql`
- `crates/store/src/db.rs`

Acceptance criteria:
- schema covers planned v1 tables and metadata fields
- migrations/open logic is explicit and testable

title: Implement HNSW index wrapper and persistence paths
description:
Objective:
Wrap HNSW / ANN index creation, persistence, and search behind a store-layer abstraction.

Why this matters:
Search should depend on a stable retrieval interface rather than vendor-specific details.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on schema and storage foundations

Planned files/modules:
- `crates/store/src/index.rs`

Acceptance criteria:
- ANN index can be opened or created for the configured dimension
- search and rebuild operations have a consistent store API

title: Implement file discovery and language dispatch
description:
Objective:
Implement repository file discovery with ignore support and language dispatch into the indexing pipeline.

Why this matters:
The vertical slice begins with accurate file selection; indexing the wrong files wastes storage and hurts result quality.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on workspace bootstrap and shared contracts

Acceptance criteria:
- supported files are discovered predictably
- ignored/generated files are skipped according to plan expectations

title: Implement initial AST chunk extraction vertical slice
description:
Objective:
Implement the first production chunk extractor vertical slice for the initial language(s), including symbol metadata, line ranges, and test detection.

Why this matters:
This is the first truly semantic content layer in the Rust implementation and determines whether downstream retrieval is meaningful.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on file discovery and core traits

Acceptance criteria:
- initial language chunks are coherent semantic units
- metadata required by search/storage is populated consistently

title: Implement embedding-text normalization
description:
Objective:
Implement the normalized embedding-text formatter that preserves raw code separately from model-facing semantic context.

Why this matters:
The project's retrieval quality depends heavily on this normalization layer.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on chunk extraction and core types

Acceptance criteria:
- raw content and embedding text remain separate fields
- formatter includes language/symbol/kind/path context as planned

title: Integrate local embedding backend
description:
Objective:
Integrate the local embedding backend for both batch indexing and single-query embedding.

Why this matters:
Local, offline-capable embedding is a core product promise and a prerequisite for any semantic retrieval.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on embedding-text normalization and embedder trait

Acceptance criteria:
- chunk batches and query strings can both be embedded via a single abstraction
- model metadata needed by storage/startup validation is available

title: Implement end-to-end indexing pipeline
description:
Objective:
Implement the indexing write path that persists files, chunks, embeddings, and ANN data coherently.

Why this matters:
This creates the durable semantic index that every command will reuse.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on schema, HNSW wrapper, chunk extraction, and embedder integration

Acceptance criteria:
- index build writes complete metadata/vector state for the MVP
- ANN rebuild or creation occurs successfully after indexing

title: Incremental indexing and graph-aware retrieval
description:
## Background
Once the MVP exists, the next constraint is operational practicality: the index must stay fresh cheaply and retrieval must understand relationships between chunks.

## Objective
Add incremental indexing correctness plus graph-aware retrieval and investigative workflows.

## Why this matters
Without these capabilities, smart-grep remains a demo rather than a daily tool for large, changing repositories.

## In scope
- file hashing and file-state transitions
- prev-vector handling
- graph extraction and graph-based search expansion/scoping
- watch mode and session workflows

## Out of scope
- broad intelligence features not dependent on these foundations

## Success Criteria
- changed-file reindex is substantially faster than full rebuild
- graph-aware commands work on fixture repos
- session workflows consolidate multi-query investigations coherently
- child issues follow naturally from the MVP rather than competing with it prematurely

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Reasoning: graph and incremental features are grouped because they upgrade the MVP from a demo into a practical daily-use tool for changing codebases and large repos.

title: Implement file-state transitions and incremental hashing
description:
Objective:
Implement file hashing, file-state transitions, and changed/deleted/unchanged handling.

Why this matters:
Incremental correctness is the prerequisite for affordable re-indexing on real repositories.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable indexing/storage foundations

Acceptance criteria:
- new/changed/deleted/unchanged states are represented explicitly
- unchanged files can be skipped safely

title: Implement prev-vector handling and rebuild strategy
description:
Objective:
Implement prev-vector snapshotting and the v1 rebuild-after-mutation strategy.

Why this matters:
Prev-vector support is required for semantic diff and stale detection, while the rebuild strategy preserves correctness for v1.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on file-state transitions and embedding persistence

Acceptance criteria:
- prior embeddings are preserved where needed before replacement
- post-mutation ANN rebuild behavior is explicit and reliable

title: Implement call-edge extraction and graph persistence
description:
Objective:
Extract call edges and persist graph relationships for the initial supported languages.

Why this matters:
Graph-aware search features all depend on reliable relationship data.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable chunk extraction and storage foundations

Acceptance criteria:
- caller/callee edges are persisted for initial languages
- graph extraction failures degrade gracefully rather than breaking search

title: Implement graph expansion and reachability-scoped search
description:
Objective:
Implement caller/callee contextual expansion and reachability-scoped search.

Why this matters:
These features reduce noise in large repos and help agents/users retrieve execution context, not isolated snippets.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on graph extraction

Acceptance criteria:
- `--expand` surfaces meaningful neighbors
- `--from` restricts search to reachable subgraphs as planned

title: Implement sessions and multi-query investigation workflows
description:
Objective:
Implement session tracking, session context assembly, and multi-query fan-out.

Why this matters:
Investigations are often multi-query; this work converts multiple related searches into durable context rather than stateless one-offs.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable search output; some context features improve further once clustering exists

Acceptance criteria:
- sessions can record and summarize searches
- multi-query search deduplicates and coverage-boosts results coherently

title: Implement watch mode on top of incremental indexing
description:
Objective:
Implement watch mode for automatic re-indexing on file changes.

Why this matters:
Watch mode makes incremental indexing operationally convenient once correctness is proven.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on incremental file-state logic and rebuild behavior

Acceptance criteria:
- file changes trigger predictable re-index actions
- watch mode avoids breaking index consistency on rapid edits

title: Packaging, docs, validation, and release readiness
description:
## Background
Even a strong engine fails adoption if installability, docs, validation, and operational quality are weak.

## Objective
Prepare smart-grep for day-to-day use and eventual release.

## Why this matters
This epic packages the engineering work into a tool that contributors and users can actually install, trust, and evaluate.

## In scope
- README/CLAUDE/AGENTS guidance
- benchmark harness and evaluation assets
- doctor/stats/release readiness
- cargo install and distribution expectations

## Out of scope
- new core retrieval ideas not required for polish

## Success Criteria
- documentation reflects the implemented command surface
- benchmark/evaluation assets are runnable
- install and health-check flows are clear and validated
- release/polish work strengthens adoption without disrupting the core feature schedule

title: Align README and agent-facing docs with the command surface
description:
Objective:
Update README and related agent-facing docs so they explain the intended smart-grep workflow, command surface, and Claude/agent integration guidance.

Why this matters:
The project is explicitly agent-oriented; its documentation should teach the right usage patterns rather than leaving contributors to infer them.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on command surface stability

Acceptance criteria:
- docs reflect implemented commands and planned usage conventions accurately
- CLAUDE/AGENTS snippets are coherent with the actual tool

title: Build benchmark and evaluation harness
description:
Objective:
Create and document the benchmark/evaluation harness that measures retrieval quality, latency, and regression risk.

Why this matters:
smart-grep's value must be demonstrated empirically and protected against future quality drift.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on the MVP search path; richer metrics benefit from later features but the harness should start early

Acceptance criteria:
- benchmark queries and expected locations are recorded
- evaluation docs explain what success looks like and how to rerun checks

title: Implement health checks, stats, and release readiness
description:
Objective:
Implement and validate operational commands such as `doctor`, `stats`, startup validation, and installability/release readiness checks.

Why this matters:
Operational trust depends on clear health checks, metadata validation, and predictable install/run behavior.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable storage/index metadata and command surface

Acceptance criteria:
- health and stats commands reflect real state clearly
- model/schema/index mismatches fail with actionable guidance
- install/release expectations are documented and tested

title: POC validation and evidence gathering
description:
## Background
This epic groups work required to validate the core premise of smart-grep before committing additional effort to optional exploratory validation. The point is to cheaply answer whether AST-derived chunks plus local embeddings can beat exact-match search for intent-style queries.

## Objective
Produce a lightweight proof of concept that demonstrates semantic retrieval value on a controlled fixture set.

## Why this matters
A successful POC can de-risk the project further, seed benchmark queries, and give future contributors concrete evidence about what retrieval patterns actually work.

## In scope
- minimal Node-based indexing/search prototype
- fixture-driven benchmarking
- documentation of what should carry forward into Rust

## Out of scope
- production-grade architecture
- broad parser support
- release packaging
- blocking the core Rust implementation path unless we explicitly decide to do so later

## Success Criteria
- concept queries return relevant matches that ripgrep misses on fixtures
- normalized embedding text is evaluated against raw-source embedding
- findings are documented for Rust carry-forward
- this track remains clearly optional/parallel rather than confusing the main implementation queue

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Planning refinement: this epic is intentionally optional/parallel. It should not crowd the primary implementation queue unless we explicitly decide to validate the concept further before Rust work.

title: Create Node POC scaffold and CLI shell
description:
Background:
The POC needs a tiny executable surface before any retrieval experiments are meaningful.

Objective:
Create the `poc/` workspace with the minimal files and command structure needed for fixture-driven experiments.

Why this matters:
This establishes a repeatable playground for validating the concept without polluting the future Rust layout.

Planned files/modules:
- `poc/package.json`
- `poc/index.js`
- `test-fixtures/`

Acceptance criteria:
- `node poc/index.js index .` and `node poc/index.js search <query>` command shells exist
- fixture locations and command expectations are documented

title: Implement JS/TS AST chunk extraction in POC
description:
Background:
The POC must prove that AST chunks are a better semantic unit than arbitrary text windows.

Objective:
Parse JS/TS fixtures with tree-sitter and extract meaningful chunks such as functions and classes.

Why this matters:
Chunk quality is the most important determinant of search quality and must be validated early.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on POC scaffold

Acceptance criteria:
- fixtures are parsed into named, line-aware semantic chunks
- line-window chunking is explicitly avoided

title: Add local embedding path and normalization experiment
description:
Background:
The POC needs a local model path consistent with the project's offline-search promise.

Objective:
Embed normalized chunk text locally and compare semantic behavior against raw-source embedding where possible.

Why this matters:
This validates both the local-inference strategy and the normalized embedding-text hypothesis.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on POC AST chunk extraction

Acceptance criteria:
- chunk embeddings are produced locally
- normalized embedding text is tested as a retrieval improvement over raw source

title: Implement POC storage and cosine retrieval loop
description:
Background:
The POC needs a minimal storage and retrieval loop to prove the end-to-end idea.

Objective:
Store chunk vectors and metadata, then run cosine-similarity search over fixture content.

Why this matters:
Without an end-to-end retrieval loop, the POC cannot produce evidence about result quality.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on POC embedding path

Acceptance criteria:
- vectors and chunk metadata are persisted for the POC
- concept queries return ranked results
- query-by-example can be exercised against stored chunks

title: Benchmark POC against ripgrep and document findings
description:
Background:
The value of the POC is in the evidence it produces, not just the code it contains.

Objective:
Benchmark POC retrieval against ripgrep on conceptual queries and record conclusions in project docs.

Why this matters:
These benchmark queries and findings should seed the permanent Rust evaluation harness and future regression checks.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on the complete POC retrieval loop

Acceptance criteria:
- at least one set of concept queries shows clear retrieval value over ripgrep
- results and carry-forward decisions are documented in `POC.md`

title: Codebase intelligence features
description:
## Background
Once the semantic index is reliable, the same substrate can power higher-order analyses that no exact-match tool can provide.

## Objective
Build codebase-intelligence features on top of embeddings, graph data, test tagging, and snapshot history.

## Why this matters
These features turn smart-grep from a search tool into a semantic understanding layer for humans and agents.

## In scope
- clustering/map
- snapshots/time-travel/semantic diff/stale detection
- semantic test coverage
- semantic linting and duplicate/orphan analysis
- session memory enhancements and project auto-detection

## Out of scope
- release/distribution work except what these features strictly require

## Success Criteria
- intelligence commands produce coherent, actionable output on realistic repos
- feature interactions are documented and testable
- intelligence work compounds on top of stable core primitives instead of forcing premature abstraction

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Reasoning: these features intentionally come after the MVP because they reuse the same semantic substrate. They should be built as compounding capabilities, not as isolated experiments.

title: Implement clustering and codebase map
description:
Objective:
Implement clustering and `smart-grep map` for codebase orientation and cluster-biased retrieval.

Why this matters:
Clustering is both a user-facing orientation feature and a reusable primitive for later confidence/session enhancements.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable embeddings across the codebase

Acceptance criteria:
- clusters can be generated and labeled coherently
- map output is useful both for humans and machine consumers

title: Implement snapshot and time-travel infrastructure
description:
Objective:
Implement snapshot save/list/restore and time-travel search against historical index state.

Why this matters:
Snapshot infrastructure is the prerequisite for meaning-level history and semantic diff workflows.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable storage/index rebuild behavior

Acceptance criteria:
- named snapshots can be saved and queried
- historical search can run against snapshot data predictably

title: Implement semantic diff and stale logic detection
description:
Objective:
Implement semantic diff and stale logic detection using current versus prior embeddings.

Why this matters:
These are signature smart-grep capabilities that justify storing prior semantic state rather than only current vectors.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on prev-vector support and snapshot infrastructure

Acceptance criteria:
- semantic changes and likely stale neighbors can be surfaced with clear thresholds/output

title: Implement semantic test coverage and gap detection
description:
Objective:
Implement semantic coverage mapping and gap detection for test-related chunks.

Why this matters:
This provides conceptual coverage insight without executing tests and showcases the value of semantic similarity beyond search.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable retrieval and test chunk tagging

Acceptance criteria:
- test-neighbor search can identify likely semantic coverage
- gap mode surfaces plausible uncovered production functions

title: Implement semantic naming and duplication linting
description:
Objective:
Implement semantic linting for naming inconsistency, duplicate logic, and orphaned code.

Why this matters:
These signals convert embedding neighborhoods into actionable codebase quality feedback.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable retrieval and high-similarity neighbor analysis

Acceptance criteria:
- lint output separates naming, duplication, and orphan signals clearly
- CI-oriented check mode is possible without excessive noise

title: Implement project auto-detection and config seeding
description:
Objective:
Implement project auto-detection and configuration seeding based on repository signatures.

Why this matters:
This lowers setup friction and helps smart-grep feel natively aware of common project layouts.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable indexing configuration concepts; can be enhanced by clustering/benchmark seeding later

Acceptance criteria:
- common repo signatures generate sensible defaults
- seeded config/query suggestions reflect detected project structure

title: Relevance, trust, and retrieval quality
description:
## Background
Base semantic retrieval is valuable, but production trust depends on ranking quality, result transparency, and graceful handling of weak matches.

## Objective
Improve search precision, explainability, and agent usability using lexical/semantic hybridization and related retrieval-quality features.

## Why this matters
A search tool that cannot explain or calibrate its output will not be trusted for real engineering decisions.

## In scope
- BM25/hybrid ranking
- query expansion and structural/example-based retrieval enhancements
- confidence calibration
- explainability and token-budget features
- streaming results

## Out of scope
- unrelated intelligence features that rely on clustering or snapshots

## Success Criteria
- exact symbol queries and intent-style queries both improve
- result explanations expose score components clearly
- low-confidence paths behave helpfully instead of returning silent noise
- features improve trust without obscuring the simpler baseline search path

title: Implement BM25 tokenizer and statistics layer
description:
Objective:
Implement the BM25 tokenizer and statistics model, including camelCase and snake_case splitting.

Why this matters:
Exact symbol queries and keyword-heavy code retrieval require a lexical signal alongside semantic similarity.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable chunk content storage

Acceptance criteria:
- BM25 state can be computed from stored chunks
- tokenizer handles identifier subword splitting as planned

title: Implement hybrid BM25 plus semantic reranking
description:
Objective:
Combine semantic retrieval with BM25 into the planned hybrid ranking pipeline.

Why this matters:
Hybrid ranking improves precision for both natural-language and exact-name queries and becomes the basis for later explainability.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on BM25 layer and baseline semantic retrieval

Acceptance criteria:
- final ranking uses explicit score components
- hybrid behavior measurably improves retrieval on mixed query styles

title: Implement synonym map and query expansion
description:
Objective:
Implement synonym-map generation and query expansion behavior.

Why this matters:
Codebases often use domain-specific vocabulary; expansion adapts retrieval to local naming conventions.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable indexing data and baseline/hybrid search

Acceptance criteria:
- concept-to-symbol relationships can be derived from indexed state
- expanded queries improve recall without flooding results

title: Implement query-by-example and structural search modes
description:
Objective:
Implement query-by-example (`--like`) and structural pattern search (`--pattern`).

Why this matters:
These modes complement natural-language retrieval and make smart-grep useful for refactoring and design-pattern discovery.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on embedding-text normalization and baseline search
- structural mode also depends on structural fingerprint extraction support

Acceptance criteria:
- code snippets can be embedded as queries
- structural fingerprints can be used to retrieve design-pattern analogues

title: Implement confidence calibration and low-confidence behavior
description:
Objective:
Implement confidence calibration and low-confidence result handling.

Why this matters:
The tool should know when it does not know; silent low-quality output undermines trust.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable ranking behavior

Acceptance criteria:
- historical score tracking supports calibrated thresholds
- warn/strict/off confidence modes behave as planned

title: Implement explainability, budget packing, and streaming output
description:
Objective:
Implement `--why`, budget packing, and streaming output features.

Why this matters:
These features make the tool legible and agent-friendly by exposing ranking rationale and packaging results for constrained contexts.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable ranking components
- `--why` is most useful once hybrid/confidence signals exist

Acceptance criteria:
- score explanations surface relevant ranking inputs
- budget mode respects token constraints predictably
- streaming output returns incremental results cleanly

title: test issue

title: Agent integration and MCP surface
description:
## Background
A major goal of smart-grep is first-class utility for coding agents, not just terminal users.

## Objective
Expose smart-grep through a stable MCP interface and agent-friendly operational modes.

## Why this matters
MCP integration is how semantic retrieval becomes a reusable tool inside Claude Code and similar systems.

## In scope
- stdio MCP server
- optional HTTP/SSE transport if retained
- tool surface mapped to existing handlers
- self-index demo mode for zero-setup exploration

## Out of scope
- redesigning core search logic specifically for MCP

## Success Criteria
- MCP server can serve the planned tools from existing backend logic
- self-index mode demonstrates the product on a fresh install
- the MCP surface stays thin and reuses core handlers instead of creating divergent behavior

title: Implement MCP server surface and transport
description:
Objective:
Implement the MCP server loop and expose the planned tool surface over stdio, with optional HTTP/SSE transport if retained.

Why this matters:
A stable MCP surface is how smart-grep becomes directly useful inside agent workflows.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable CLI/backend handlers for the features being exposed

Acceptance criteria:
- MCP server can serve the planned tools using existing backend logic
- transport behavior is documented and testable

title: Wire backend handlers into MCP tools
description:
Objective:
Wire existing search/context/intelligence handlers into the MCP tool API without duplicating business logic.

Why this matters:
The MCP layer should be a transport/interface layer, not a second implementation of search features.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on MCP server scaffold and the relevant backend features existing

Acceptance criteria:
- MCP tools reuse internal handlers consistently
- response schemas expose the metadata agents need

title: Implement self-index demo mode
description:
Objective:
Implement `--self` mode by bundling a prebuilt index of the smart-grep codebase or equivalent self-demo substrate.

Why this matters:
Self-index mode doubles as an instant demo and as semantic contributor documentation.

Dependencies/assumptions:
- depends on stable indexing/search behavior and build-time packaging decisions

Acceptance criteria:
- fresh installs can run `--self` queries without first indexing another repo
- self-demo behavior is documented and reproducible
